fields.brief_block: Courts require that the conduct both alarm others and provoke an actual, not merely speculative, breach of peace. Thayer v. Chiczewski, 705 F.3d 237, 248–49 (7th Cir. 2012).
fields.opinion_block: To commit disorderly conduct, “a person must engage in conduct that: (1) is unreasonable; (2) alarms or disturbs another; and (3) threatens to provoke or provokes a breach of the peace.”
fields.finding_analysis:
The opinion does support the dual-requirement core of the proposition. Quoting Reher and the Illinois disorderly conduct statute (720 ILCS 5/26-1(a)(1)) at pages 248-49, the court states that disorderly conduct requires conduct that both “alarms or disturbs another” and provokes a breach of the peace, joined by “and,” so both elements are necessary.

The brief overstates the breach-of-the-peace element, however. The brief demands “an actual, not merely speculative, breach of the peace,” but the court's own formulation is that the conduct “threatens to provoke or provokes a breach of the peace.” Because a mere threat to provoke suffices under the opinion, the case does not require an actual rather than speculative breach, making the brief's gloss an overstatement of what Thayer holds.

fields.brief_block: Moreover, as Illinois courts have held and the Seventh Circuit confirmed, peaceful picketing, placard-carrying, and chanting cannot form the basis of disorderly conduct charges. Id.
fields.opinion_block: “[S]peech alone cannot form the basis for a disorderly conduct charge.” ... “It remains no crime to express an unpopular view even if the person expressing those views draws attention to herself or himself or annoys others nearby.”
fields.finding_analysis:
The opinion supports the general principle underlying the proposition: it holds that “speech alone cannot form the basis for a disorderly conduct charge” and that it “remains no crime to express an unpopular view even if the person expressing those views draws attention to herself or himself or annoys others nearby,” quoting Rokicki and Raby (the latter noting persons may not be punished “merely for peacefully expressing unpopular views”).

The brief extends that principle in two ways the opinion does not squarely endorse. It enumerates specific protest activities — picketing, placard-carrying, and chanting — that the opinion never lists, and it frames the rule as absolute. The same opinion recognizes that blocking the free flow of pedestrian or vehicular traffic can support a disorderly conduct conviction (citing Jones v. Watson and Marcavage), so peaceful picketing that obstructs a sidewalk is not categorically immune. The case supports the core idea but not the brief's absolute, enumerated formulation.

fields.brief_block: In Thayer v. Chiczewski, 705 F.3d at 247, both senior police officers testified under oath that a sidewalk press conference requires no permit at all.
fields.opinion_block: Officer Chiczewski testified, however, that depending on the circumstances, no permit was needed to assemble and hold a press conference at that location. ... he [Officer Killackey] was aware that no permit was needed to hold an assembly or press conference on the sidewalk (as opposed to a “parade” down the street).
fields.finding_analysis:
The opinion supports the proposition. It recounts that both of the senior officers in command testified that no permit was required for a sidewalk press conference or assembly: Officer Chiczewski (Deputy Chief of the Central Control Group) testified that “depending on the circumstances, no permit was needed to assemble and hold a press conference at that location,” and Officer Killackey (Deputy Chief of Area 1 Patrol) testified that he “was aware that no permit was needed to hold an assembly or press conference on the sidewalk (as opposed to a ‘parade’ down the street).”

The only imprecision is the pinpoint: this testimony appears in the opinion's factual narrative at pages 244-45, not at page 247 as cited. The substance of the proposition is accurate.

fields.brief_block: And as the Seventh Circuit recognized in Thayer v. Chiczewski, 705 F.3d at 246, Chicago officers may regulate specific individual acts of traffic obstruction without issuing a blanket dispersal order against an entire assembly.
fields.finding_analysis:
Thayer v. Chiczewski is a Section 1983 qualified-immunity case about officers who issued a blanket order of dispersal to an entire antiwar assembly, formed a police line, and arrested those who failed to comply; the court held the officers had arguable probable cause and were entitled to qualified immunity. It does not recognize the principle the brief attributes to it.

Nothing in the opinion states that officers may regulate specific individual acts of traffic obstruction without issuing a blanket dispersal order against an assembly. If anything the opinion runs the other way: it approves the officers' use of a blanket dispersal order directed at the whole crowd as “the most logical way” to manage the situation. The cited page 246 falls within the factual account of Lyttle's arrest and contains no such holding. The proposition ascribes a rule the case does not contain.

fields.brief_block: Even where a dispersal order is valid, police must have individualized probable cause to believe each arrested person (1) heard the dispersal order, (2) understood it, and (3) willfully defied it. Vodak, 639 F.3d at 748; Barham v. Ramsey, 434 F.3d 565, 573 (D.C. Cir. 2006).
fields.opinion_block: Dellums held that a group of demonstrators lawfully gathered on the Capitol steps could not be arrested unless the Chief had reason to believe (1) that the demonstrators could be validly evicted under the Capitol Grounds ordinance, (2) that the police gave demonstrators an order to disperse that “apprised the crowd as a whole that it was under an obligation to leave,” and (3) that there had been a “reasonable opportunity” to comply. ... the “fair notice” required by the Cullinane court is notice reasonably likely to have reached all of the crowd despite any noise the demonstrators may have been making.
fields.finding_analysis:
Barham v. Ramsey is a Fourth Amendment qualified-immunity case about a mass arrest of anti-globalization demonstrators in Pershing Park. It addresses the brief's general topic, holding that an officer cannot sweep up an “undifferentiated mass” of people absent probable cause “particularized with respect to” each person, and that police may arrest a crowd as a unit only after issuing a valid dispersal order with “fair notice” reasonably likely to reach the whole crowd and a reasonable opportunity to comply.

The gap is that the brief recasts those principles into a specific three-element test — individualized probable cause that each arrestee “heard,” “understood,” and “willfully defied” the order — that the opinion never articulates. Barham requires particularized probable cause for the offense of arrest and, separately, a valid order plus fair notice and opportunity to comply; it does not frame the notice-and-defiance elements as components of “individualized probable cause,” and “understood it” in particular has no counterpart in the text. Because the Pershing Park officers issued no dispersal order at all, the court never reached the question of what probable cause is needed to believe a particular person heard and defied an order. The proposition is a reasonable extension of the dispersal-order and particularized-probable-cause holdings, but it overstates and over-formalizes what the case actually decided.

fields.brief_block: This is the forbidden "heckler's veto." Gregory v. City of Chicago, 394 U.S. at 125 (Black, J., concurring); Goldhamer v. Nagode, 621 F.3d 581 (7th Cir. 2010).
fields.opinion_block: this provision can be applied to impose what amounts to an unconstitutional 'heckler's veto' of protected speech.
fields.finding_analysis: Goldhamer expressly endorses the heckler's-veto principle the brief cites it for, observing that the failure-to-disperse provision 'can be applied to impose what amounts to an unconstitutional "heckler's veto" of protected speech' and that it 'can be misused to impose a heckler's veto or otherwise to suppress speech and expressive conduct protected by the First Amendment.' Although the Seventh Circuit ultimately resolved the case on standing and expressly did not decide the ordinance's constitutionality, it plainly recognizes the doctrine that a hostile audience's reaction cannot justify dispersing peaceful protest, so the citation accurately supports the general proposition.

fields.brief_block: In Goldhamer v. Nagode, the Seventh Circuit addressed a nearly identical situation [...]. The court held that Chicago Municipal Code § 8-4-010(d) cannot be applied as a heckler's veto and that the arrests were apparently without probable cause. 621 F.3d at 588–90.
fields.opinion_block: We do not address this provision's constitutionality because we conclude that the plaintiffs lack standing to challenge its facial validity.
fields.finding_analysis:
The second half of this sentence is well grounded but the first half overstates the holding. The opinion repeatedly describes the plaintiffs' arrests as baseless and made 'without even arguable probable cause,' and the protest (peaceful leafletting near a recruiting booth) is indeed closely analogous, so the assertion that the arrests were apparently without probable cause is accurate.

But the court did not 'hold' that section 8-4-010(d) 'cannot be applied as a heckler's veto.' It expressly declined to reach the provision's constitutionality, resolving the case on standing grounds: 'We do not address this provision's constitutionality because we conclude that the plaintiffs lack standing to challenge its facial validity.' The heckler's-veto discussion is acknowledged only as a risk the provision could pose, not adjudicated as a holding, so attributing such a holding to the court overstates the decision.

fields.brief_block: The Seventh Circuit in Goldhamer v. Nagode found that when "the police appear to have had no arguable probable cause" based on the predicate disorderly conduct, arrests for failure to disperse are actionable under § 1983. 621 F.3d at 591.
fields.opinion_block: Nevertheless, the police arrested these plaintiffs and had them charged with failure to disperse without even arguable probable cause for doing so.
fields.finding_analysis:
The quoted phrase 'the police appear to have had no arguable probable cause' does not appear in the opinion; it is a paraphrase set in quotation marks. The court's actual language is that the plaintiffs were charged 'without even arguable probable cause' and that their 'arrests appear to have been baseless.'

On substance, however, the proposition is supported. The opinion treats a damages action as the proper remedy for exactly this scenario, noting that the plaintiffs 'had standing to pursue their claims for damages based on what may well have been unlawful arrests,' and that 'a claim for money damages ... is an appropriate and effective method for dealing with isolated circumstances in which police officers arrest civilians who are not even arguably violating the law.' The plaintiffs' suit was brought under 42 U.S.C. § 1983. The pinpoint to page 591 falls outside the opinion's range (581–589), but the supporting discussion appears at pages 585 and 588.

fields.brief_block: Similarly, in Gregory v. City of Chicago, 394 U.S. 111 (1969), the Supreme Court unanimously reversed disorderly conduct convictions of peaceful civil rights protesters in Chicago who marched to Mayor Daley's home, even though hostile bystanders threw rocks and eggs and police feared an imminent riot. The Court held that the demonstrators' own peaceful conduct could not form the basis of a criminal conviction.
fields.opinion_block: Petitioners' march, if peaceful and orderly, falls well within the sphere of conduct protected by the First Amendment. . . . There is no evidence in this record that petitioners' conduct was disorderly. . . . the onlookers became unruly as the number of bystanders increased.
fields.finding_analysis:
Gregory v. City of Chicago reverses the disorderly-conduct convictions of peaceful civil-rights marchers, holding that petitioners' march, if peaceful and orderly, falls well within the sphere of First Amendment protection and that there was no evidence in the record that their conduct was disorderly. That directly supports the proposition that peaceful demonstrators' own conduct cannot supply the basis for a criminal conviction even where police feared disorder.

The opinion describes the crowd only as onlookers who became unruly; it does not say bystanders threw rocks and eggs, so that factual detail in the brief is not drawn from this opinion. It does not undermine the legal point the case is cited for.

fields.brief_block: Justice Black's concurrence emphasized: "To let a policeman's command become equivalent to a criminal statute comes dangerously near making our government one of men rather than of laws." Id. at 125.
fields.finding_analysis: The brief attributes this quotation to Justice Black's concurrence at page 125, but the file provided contains only Chief Justice Warren's majority opinion, which runs through page 113; the concurrence is not included. The quoted language appears nowhere in the majority opinion, and the concurring opinion the brief relies on is absent from the supplied text, so its content can be neither confirmed nor refuted from this file.

fields.brief_block: This is the forbidden "heckler's veto." Gregory v. City of Chicago, 394 U.S. at 125 (Black, J., concurring); Goldhamer v. Nagode, 621 F.3d 581 (7th Cir. 2010).
fields.finding_analysis: This proposition is pinned to Justice Black's concurrence at page 125, which is not contained in the provided file; the file holds only the majority opinion, which ends at page 113 and nowhere uses the phrase "heckler's veto" or addresses whether police may disperse a peaceful assembly because of a hostile crowd's reaction. Because the concurring opinion the brief relies on is missing from the supplied text, the heckler's-veto proposition cannot be verified against this file.

fields.brief_block: The "potential for escalation" is precisely the speculative, subjective concern that Edwards v. South Carolina and Gregory v. City of Chicago held to be constitutionally insufficient.
fields.opinion_block: However reasonable the police request may have been and however laudable the police motives, petitioners were charged and convicted for holding a demonstration, not for a refusal to obey a police officer.
fields.finding_analysis: Gregory is topically on point -- peaceful marchers were arrested after police, fearing an impending civil disorder, ordered them to disperse, and the Court reversed -- but it does not hold that a speculative concern about escalation is constitutionally insufficient to justify dispersing a peaceful assembly. The Court rested its reversal on the absence of any evidence of disorderly conduct and on the fact that petitioners were convicted for demonstrating rather than for refusing a police order, and it expressly declined to pass on the validity of the dispersal demand. The brief extends the holding beyond what the case decided.

fields.brief_block: It sits at the zenith of constitutional protection under Hague v. CIO, Edwards v. South Carolina, and Gregory v. City of Chicago.
fields.opinion_block: Petitioners' march, if peaceful and orderly, falls well within the sphere of conduct protected by the First Amendment.
fields.finding_analysis: Gregory supports the general point that peaceful political protest is firmly protected, holding that petitioners' peaceful and orderly march falls well within the sphere of conduct protected by the First Amendment. But it does not analyze public-forum doctrine, does not involve a downtown plaza, and does not characterize any location as a traditional public forum or speak of a zenith of protection. Offered in a string alongside Hague, the public-forum case, Gregory contributes only the proposition that peaceful protest is highly protected, not the forum-based framing the sentence advances, so it partially supports the proposition.

fields.brief_block: As Justice Roberts wrote in Hague v. Committee for Industrial Organization, 307 U.S. 496, 515 (1939) — the foundational case establishing the public forum doctrine: "Streets and parks … have immemorially been held in trust for the use of the public and, time out of mind, have been used for purposes of assembly, communicating thoughts between citizens, and discussing public questions."
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion file provided contains only Hague's one-paragraph per curiam disposition, which announces that "the decree is modified and as modified affirmed" and notes that "Mr. Justice Roberts has an opinion in which Mr. Justice Black concurs." The body of Justice Roberts's opinion — where the cited public-forum language about streets and parks being "held in trust for the use of the public" appears — is not included in the text. Because the substantive passage the brief quotes and attributes to page 515 is absent from the document supplied, the proposition cannot be verified against this text.

fields.brief_block: It sits at the zenith of constitutional protection under Hague v. CIO, Edwards v. South Carolina, and Gregory v. City of Chicago.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion file provided contains only Hague's one-paragraph per curiam disposition and a note that Justice Roberts authored a separate opinion; it does not include the substantive discussion of public forums, traditional assembly rights, or the degree of protection afforded political expression in such spaces. Because none of the text grounding the brief's "zenith of constitutional protection in a traditional public forum" proposition is present in the document supplied, the proposition cannot be verified against this text.

fields.brief_block: [B]oth individual officers and the municipality (through final policymaking authority under Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)) may be liable.
fields.opinion_block: Instead, it is when execution of a government's policy or custom, whether made by its lawmakers or by those whose edicts or acts may fairly be said to represent official policy, inflicts the injury that the government as an entity is responsible under § 1983.
fields.finding_analysis: Monell v. Department of Social Services is the foundational § 1983 municipal-liability decision, and it directly supports the proposition that a municipality may be liable through its final policymaking authority. The Court overruled Monroe v. Pape insofar as it immunized local governments, held that municipalities are "persons" suable under § 1983, and limited that liability to injuries inflicted by "execution of a government's policy or custom, whether made by its lawmakers or by those whose edicts or acts may fairly be said to represent official policy." The brief's "final policymaking authority" framing is the conventional shorthand for that holding (later refined in cases like Pembaur and Praprotnik), and it accurately captures Monell's core rule that municipal liability attaches to official policy rather than respondeat superior.

fields.brief_block: The Supreme Court has repeatedly held that the First Amendment's core purpose is to protect political dissent, particularly speech directed at government policy. NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co., 458 U.S. 886, 907–09 (1982) (political protest and boycotts protected even when government policy is the target).
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion directly supports the cited-for assertion that political protest and boycotts are protected even when government policy is the target. At the pinpointed pages 907–09 (Part II.A), the Court canvasses the protected elements of the boycott—speech, assembly, association, and petition—and quotes De Jonge v. Oregon for the principle that peaceable assembly for lawful political discussion cannot be made a crime. The Court emphasizes that a major purpose of the boycott was to influence governmental action and holds that 'the nonviolent elements of petitioners' activities are entitled to the protection of the First Amendment,' and that the State could not prohibit 'a nonviolent, politically motivated boycott designed to force governmental and economic change.' This squarely grounds the proposition that politically motivated boycotts and protest aimed at changing government policy retain First Amendment protection.

fields.brief_block: The Court stated that speech retains First Amendment protection even when it "induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction with conditions as they are, or even stirs people to anger." Id. at 237 (quoting Terminiello v. City of Chicago, 337 U.S. 1 (1949)).
fields.finding_analysis: Terminiello v. Chicago is the source of this exact language, and the cited proposition tracks the opinion's holding. Justice Douglas wrote that "a function of free speech under our system of government is to invite dispute," and that speech "may indeed best serve its high purpose when it induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction with conditions as they are, or even stirs people to anger," remaining "protected against censorship or punishment, unless shown likely to produce a clear and present danger of a serious substantive evil that rises far above public inconvenience, annoyance, or unrest." The quoted phrase appears verbatim in the opinion and is used for the precise proposition the brief attributes to it.

fields.brief_block: Even in Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, 744 (7th Cir. 2011) — where the court upheld the authority to issue a dispersal order — the justification involved 8,000 protesters occupying major arterial roadways, some marchers banging on cars in rush-hour traffic, and shouts suggesting a threat to a commercial district. The Seventh Circuit described that as a march that was "getting out of hand." Id.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion supports this characterization. Posner wrote that "the authority of the police to order the crowd to disperse and return to its starting point cannot be questioned," in circumstances involving as many as 8,000 marchers who had blocked Lake Shore Drive (a multilane commuting thoroughfare) and were pressing toward Michigan Avenue, with earlier rowdiness in which marchers rushed into traffic lanes "banging on the hoods and windows of cars" and shouts of "Take Michigan!" The court described the situation by saying "the march was now getting out of hand; traffic was being impeded," so the quoted phrase and the supporting facts both track the opinion.

fields.brief_block: The Seventh Circuit in Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d at 747, expressly applied this rule: "Before the police could start arresting peaceable demonstrators for defying their orders, they had to communicate the orders to the demonstrators."
fields.finding_analysis: Directly supported. The opinion states: "But before the police could start arresting peaceable demonstrators for defying their orders they had to communicate the orders to the demonstrators." The brief's quotation is verbatim but for an inconsequential added comma.

fields.brief_block: The court further held that arrests are unlawful where there is no "good reason to believe that the protesters who were arrested, or at least most of them, had heard the orders." Id. at 748.
fields.opinion_block: And even if dispersal orders were given, there would have to be evidence that the police reasonably believed that the protesters who were arrested, or at least most of them, had heard the orders. For this could not be assumed.
fields.finding_analysis: The substance is supported: the opinion holds that arrests cannot stand absent a reasonable basis to believe the arrestees, or at least most of them, had heard the dispersal orders. The quotation, however, is reworded rather than verbatim. The opinion's actual phrasing is that "there would have to be evidence that the police reasonably believed that the protesters who were arrested, or at least most of them, had heard the orders"; the phrase "good reason to believe" comes from a separate sentence addressing whether the police could arrest people they had "no good reason to believe knew they were violating a police order." The brief has stitched the two together, but the underlying proposition holds.

fields.brief_block: Even where a dispersal order is valid, police must have individualized probable cause to believe each arrested person (1) heard the dispersal order, (2) understood it, and (3) willfully defied it. Vodak, 639 F.3d at 748; Barham v. Ramsey, 434 F.3d 565, 573 (D.C. Cir. 2006).
fields.finding_analysis: Supported. The opinion holds that the police needed probable cause and that "[w]hat they could not lawfully do ... was arrest people who the police had no good reason to believe knew they were violating a police order," and that there must be evidence the police "reasonably believed that the protesters who were arrested, or at least most of them, had heard the orders." The brief's tripartite formulation (heard, understood, willfully defied) is a fair synthesis of the opinion's requirement of an individualized basis to believe each arrestee heard and knowingly violated the order.

fields.brief_block: The fact that some individuals in a group may have been disorderly does not supply probable cause to arrest all individuals, including those who were peaceably assembled and had not necessarily received the order. Vodak, 639 F.3d at 748–49 ("The fact that some of the marchers were rowdy and may have committed criminal acts … need not be discussed in detail; demonstrators' misconduct cannot … be thought to have justified the mass arrests of [others].").
fields.finding_analysis: Directly supported. The opinion states that "the fact that some of the marchers were rowdy and may have committed criminal acts ... need not be discussed in detail; demonstrators' misconduct cannot on the present record be thought to have justified the mass arrests of persons trapped by the police on Chicago Avenue." The brief's quotation accurately reproduces this passage with ellipses and a bracketed substitution for "persons trapped by the police on Chicago Avenue."

fields.brief_block: [T]he government cannot entrap citizens "by convicting a citizen for exercising a privilege which the State had clearly told him was available to him." Id. at 746 (quoting Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. at 571).
fields.finding_analysis: Supported. The opinion, quoting Cox v. Louisiana, characterizes arresting demonstrators who were told they could march as "an indefensible sort of entrapment by the State — convicting a citizen for exercising a privilege which the State had clearly told him was available to him." The brief's quoted language tracks the opinion (the opinion omits the brief's introductory "by," but the substance is identical).

fields.brief_block: As the Seventh Circuit noted in Vodak, it would be "an indefensible sort of entrapment by the State" to first allow lawful conduct, then issue a dispersal order, and immediately arrest those who did not comply. 639 F.3d at 746.
fields.finding_analysis: Supported. The opinion applies the "indefensible sort of entrapment by the State" principle to precisely this sequence, stating that the Fourth Amendment does not permit police "to say to a person go ahead and march and then, five minutes later, having revoked the permission for the march without notice to anyone, arrest the person for having marched without police permission." The quoted phrase is verbatim.

fields.brief_block: See Vodak, 639 F.3d at 750 (Cox v. Louisiana established notice requirement "decades" before 2003, meaning qualified immunity was unavailable then — a fortiori by 2025).
fields.finding_analysis: The narrower cited-for assertion is supported. The court rejected qualified immunity because the law was clearly established well before the 2003 demonstration: "The Supreme Court had held decades earlier that police must give notice of revocation of permission to demonstrate before they can begin arresting demonstrators," citing Cox v. Louisiana. The quoted word "decades" is verbatim. (The brief's pinpoint to page 750 is off — the discussion appears at 746–47 — and the "a fortiori by 2025" inference is the brief's own extrapolation, but the assertion judged here tracks the opinion.)

fields.brief_block: Under Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. 536, and Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, police must clearly communicate a dispersal order to each individual and must have individualized probable cause to believe each arrestee heard and willfully defied the order.
fields.finding_analysis: Supported as to Vodak. The opinion requires that police "communicate the orders to the demonstrators" before arresting them, that there be evidence police "reasonably believed that the protesters who were arrested, or at least most of them, had heard the orders," and that they not arrest people they had "no good reason to believe knew they were violating a police order." That is the individualized, communicated-and-heard standard the brief states.

fields.brief_block: Under the standard articulated in Ward v. Rock Against Racism, 491 U.S. 781, 791 (1989), and Clark v. Community for Creative Non-Violence, 468 U.S. 288, 293 (1984), even content-neutral time, place, and manner restrictions are constitutionally valid only if they are: [content-neutral; narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest; and leave open ample alternative channels].
fields.finding_analysis: Ward supplies exactly this rule at page 791, holding that even in a public forum the government may impose reasonable time, place, or manner restrictions provided they "are justified without reference to the content of the regulated speech, that they are narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest, and that they leave open ample alternative channels for communication of the information." The brief's three-element formulation tracks the opinion accurately, and the pinpoint to page 791 is correct.

fields.brief_block: A content-based restriction triggers strict scrutiny and is presumptively unconstitutional. Even content-neutral dispersal orders must satisfy each of these three prongs. Ward, 491 U.S. at 791.
fields.finding_analysis: Ward supports both halves of this proposition. The page 791 holding sets out the three-prong content-neutral time, place, and manner test, and footnote 6 confirms that content-based restrictions on political speech "must be subjected to the most exacting scrutiny," which the brief fairly renders as strict scrutiny. The brief's reference to "dispersal orders" is its own application context rather than the facts of Ward (a sound-amplification guideline), but the underlying legal standards the brief attributes to the case are stated by the opinion.

fields.brief_block: Even analyzing the dispersal order as a content-neutral time, place, and manner restriction under Ward v. Rock Against Racism, 491 U.S. 781, it must be narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest and must leave open ample alternative channels.
fields.finding_analysis: This is the Ward standard in substance: the opinion at page 791 and in Sections II.B and II.C requires that a content-neutral regulation be "narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest" and "leave open ample alternative channels for communication of the information." The brief's application to a dispersal order is its own context, but the requirements it attributes to Ward are stated by the opinion.

fields.brief_block: Even as a content-neutral restriction, the blanket order to disperse a 200-person peaceful protest entirely — rather than narrowly targeting individual acts of actual obstruction — was not narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest under Ward v. Rock Against Racism, 491 U.S. 781.
fields.opinion_block: a regulation of the time, place, or manner of protected speech must be narrowly tailored to serve the government's legitimate, content-neutral interests but that it need not be the least restrictive or least intrusive means of doing so.
fields.finding_analysis:
Ward supplies the narrow-tailoring standard the brief invokes, but it does not hold that a blanket dispersal order fails that standard, and its central holding cuts partly against the brief's framing. Ward concerns a municipal sound-amplification guideline, not crowd dispersal, and the Court expressly rejected the "least intrusive means" requirement, holding that a content-neutral regulation "need not be the least restrictive or least intrusive means" of serving the government's interest. The brief's argument that officers should have "narrowly target[ed] individual acts" instead of dispersing the whole assembly is a less-restrictive-alternative argument of the kind Ward disapproved.

Ward does recognize the outer limit the brief gestures toward — a regulation may not "burden substantially more speech than is necessary" to advance the government's goals — so the narrow-tailoring standard is correctly stated. But the specific conclusion that a blanket dispersal order flunks narrow tailoring is the brief's own extension and application, not a holding of Ward.

fields.brief_block: The Supreme Court in Brandenburg v. Ohio, 395 U.S. 444, 447 (1969), further established that advocacy of general ideas — including advocacy against government immigration policy — retains full First Amendment protection unless it is "directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action and is likely to incite or produce such action."
fields.finding_analysis: Brandenburg directly supports the proposition: the per curiam opinion states that "the constitutional guarantees of free speech and free press do not permit a State to forbid or proscribe advocacy of the use of force or of law violation except where such advocacy is directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action and is likely to incite or produce such action," and contrasts protected "mere advocacy" with unprotected "incitement to imminent lawless action." The quoted language is verbatim, and the brief's gloss that abstract advocacy retains full protection absent the incitement showing accurately states the holding. The brief's added illustration about immigration-policy advocacy is its own application and is not attributed to the case.

fields.brief_block: The Brandenburg v. Ohio, 395 U.S. 444 (1969), standard — requiring speech directed to and likely to produce imminent lawless action — was nowhere close to being met on these facts.
fields.finding_analysis: The brief accurately characterizes the Brandenburg standard, which the opinion frames as requiring that advocacy be "directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action and is likely to incite or produce such action" before it may be proscribed. The clause about the standard being "nowhere close to being met on these facts" refers to the brief's own case, not to Brandenburg, and is context rather than an assertion attributed to the cited opinion; judged on the standard it invokes, the citation is supported.

fields.brief_block: However, the Seventh Circuit in Bell v. Keating, 697 F.3d 445 (7th Cir. 2012), partially struck down § 8-4-010(d) as facially overbroad and unconstitutionally vague. The court: severed and invalidated the "serious inconvenience, annoyance or alarm" triggers; upheld only the "substantial harm" trigger as constitutionally valid.
fields.finding_analysis: Bell v. Keating directly and accurately supports this. The Seventh Circuit announced that it would "partially invalidate the ordinance," holding Subsection D both facially overbroad under the First Amendment and void for vagueness under the Fourteenth. After declining total invalidation, the court declared unconstitutional the phrase "or serious inconvenience, annoyance, or alarm" while leaving intact the City's authority to order dispersal where three or more persons commit disorderly conduct "likely to cause substantial harm." The brief's description of the severance and of the surviving "substantial harm" trigger tracks the holding exactly.

fields.brief_block: The court held that "avoiding annoyance is never a proper basis on which to curtail protected speech," citing Coates v. City of Cincinnati, 402 U.S. 611 (1971). Bell v. Keating, 697 F.3d at 454.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion states verbatim, "Avoiding annoyance is never a proper basis on which to curtail protected speech," and supports it with a parenthetical citation to Coates v. City of Cincinnati, 402 U.S. at 615 — precisely as the brief represents. The only discrepancy is the pinpoint: the statement appears in the court's discussion of dispersal on the basis of annoyance at page 460, not at page 454 as the brief cites. The substance of the proposition is fully supported.

fields.brief_block: The "alarm" and "annoyance" terms were struck as both overbroad (encumbering substantially more constitutionally protected expression than legitimate conduct) and unconstitutionally vague (providing officers with standardless discretion and failing to give citizens adequate notice). Id. at 455–58.
fields.finding_analysis: The Seventh Circuit struck the "annoyance" and "alarm" triggers on both grounds the brief identifies. On overbreadth, it held that dispersal on the basis of annoyance "substantially encumbers protected expression" and that the alarm trigger is overbroad to the extent the related terms are. On vagueness, it faulted "annoyance" as predicating penalty "on an inscrutable standard, which is no standard at all," leaving individuals without notice and inviting "unbridled discretion," and treated "alarm" as coextensive with the other defective terms so that it "suffers from whatever imprecision afflicts them." The 455-58 pinpoint captures the opening of the overbreadth analysis; the parallel vagueness reasoning continues through page 463, but the brief's characterization is accurate.

fields.brief_block: Under Chicago Municipal Code § 8-4-010(d), as narrowed by Bell v. Keating, 697 F.3d 445 (7th Cir. 2012), a lawful dispersal order requires three or more persons in the immediate vicinity committing disorderly conduct likely to cause substantial harm.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion's narrowing holding matches the brief exactly. After striking the other triggers, the court held that the City may criminalize one's failure "to obey a lawful order of dispersal by a person known by him to be a peace officer under circumstances where three or more persons are committing acts of disorderly conduct in the immediate vicinity, which acts are likely to cause substantial harm." That is precisely the narrowed standard the brief describes.

fields.brief_block: Verbal disagreement is not a "fighting words" situation under Chaplinsky v. New Hampshire, 315 U.S. 568 (1942), absent actual, face-to-face provocations of an immediate breach of the peace.
fields.opinion_block: These include the lewd and obscene, the profane, the libelous, and the insulting or “fighting” words — those which by their very utterance inflict injury or tend to incite an immediate breach of the peace. . . . The statute, as construed, does no more than prohibit the face-to-face words plainly likely to cause a breach of the peace by the addressee.
fields.finding_analysis: Chaplinsky v. New Hampshire is a First Amendment case defining the narrow "fighting words" exception to free speech, and it directly supports the brief's proposition. The Court limited the category to words "which by their very utterance inflict injury or tend to incite an immediate breach of the peace," and upheld the statute only as construed to reach "the face-to-face words plainly likely to cause a breach of the peace by the addressee." The brief's negative inference—that mere verbal disagreement, absent actual face-to-face provocation of an immediate breach of the peace, is not fighting words—follows directly from that definition, and the quoted phrase "fighting words" appears verbatim in the opinion.

fields.brief_block: Under the standard articulated in Ward v. Rock Against Racism, 491 U.S. 781, 791 (1989), and Clark v. Community for Creative Non-Violence, 468 U.S. 288, 293 (1984), even content-neutral time, place, and manner restrictions are constitutionally valid only if they are: [content-neutral; narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest; and leave open ample alternative channels].
fields.opinion_block: We have often noted that restrictions of this kind are valid provided that they are justified without reference to the content of the regulated speech, that they are narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest, and that they leave open ample alternative channels for communication of the information.
fields.finding_analysis: Clark v. Community for Creative Non-Violence directly supports the proposition: the opinion recites the three-part time, place, and manner standard at the cited page 293, stating that such restrictions are valid only when justified without reference to content (content-neutral), narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest, and leaving open ample alternative channels for communication. The brief's articulation tracks the opinion's holding accurately, and the pinpoint citation to page 293 is correct.

fields.brief_block: The court held that "avoiding annoyance is never a proper basis on which to curtail protected speech," citing Coates v. City of Cincinnati, 402 U.S. 611 (1971). Bell v. Keating, 697 F.3d at 454.
fields.opinion_block: The First and Fourteenth Amendments do not permit a State to make criminal the exercise of the right of assembly simply because its exercise may be “annoying” to some people.
fields.finding_analysis:
Coates squarely supports the narrower proposition that avoiding annoyance is not a proper basis for curtailing protected speech or assembly. The Court struck down a Cincinnati ordinance criminalizing conduct “annoying to persons passing by” as both unconstitutionally vague and overbroad, holding that the First and Fourteenth Amendments do not permit a State to make criminal the exercise of the right of assembly simply because its exercise may be annoying to some people, and that mere public intolerance or animosity cannot be the basis for abridgment of these freedoms.

The substance is therefore well grounded in the cited opinion. The only gap is one of quotation form: the exact words “avoiding annoyance is never a proper basis on which to curtail protected speech” are the Seventh Circuit's formulation in Bell v. Keating, not language that appears in the Coates opinion itself. The brief's framing is accurate in attributing the underlying principle to Coates, but the quoted phrase is a reworded paraphrase rather than Coates's own text.

fields.brief_block: Moreover, the Supreme Court confirmed in Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. 536, 554–55 (1965), that even where traffic regulations are validly implicated, the government cannot use them as a pretext to suppress protected political expression.
fields.finding_analysis: Cox reverses appellant's obstructing-public-passages conviction because Baton Rouge applied its street-obstruction statute through standardless, unfettered official discretion that functioned as the equivalent of a discretionary licensing scheme — a 'device for the suppression of the communication of ideas' — even while the Court acknowledged the State's legitimate power to regulate streets for traffic and safety. The opinion squarely supports the proposition, holding the practice 'an unwarranted abridgment of appellant's freedom of speech and assembly secured to him by the First Amendment.' The 554–55 pinpoint correctly frames the street-regulation-versus-free-speech issue the Court resolved against the State.

fields.brief_block: The Supreme Court established in Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. 571, 571–73 (1965), that police must give notice of a dispersal order and must not begin arresting demonstrators until that notice has been communicated.
fields.finding_analysis: The brief cites Cox v. Louisiana at 379 U.S. 571, which is the separate companion opinion (No. 49, the courthouse-picketing case), but the opinion provided here is the distinct No. 24 majority reported at 379 U.S. 536, whose star-pagination ends at page 558 and which closes by directing the reader to the later opinions beginning at page 575 and 591. Page 571 and the cited 571–73 range are not contained in this document, so the provided text cannot ground the dispersal-order-notice proposition. For what it is worth, the 536 opinion reverses on free-speech and vagueness grounds and announces no rule requiring police to communicate a dispersal order before arresting; the legal effect of the order is among the matters this opinion defers to No. 49.

fields.brief_block: [T]he government cannot entrap citizens "by convicting a citizen for exercising a privilege which the State had clearly told him was available to him." Id. at 746 (quoting Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. at 571).
fields.finding_analysis: The brief cites Cox v. Louisiana at 379 U.S. 571 for the privilege/entrapment principle, but the file provided is the No. 24 opinion at 379 U.S. 536, which ends at page 558 and does not include page 571 — that pinpoint belongs to the companion No. 49 opinion. The provided 536 opinion's footnote 3 confirms the mismatch, stating that the 'permission' granted to the students 'is discussed at greater length in No. 49, where its legal effect is considered'; the holding the brief invokes thus lives in the companion opinion not contained in this file, so the proposition cannot be verified from the text supplied. The quoted language was also independently flagged FABRICATED.

fields.brief_block: Under Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. 536, and Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, police must clearly communicate a dispersal order to each individual and must have individualized probable cause to believe each arrestee heard and willfully defied the order.
fields.finding_analysis: Cox v. Louisiana (379 U.S. 536) is a First Amendment case reversing a single demonstration leader's convictions for disturbing the peace (on free-speech and vagueness grounds) and for obstructing public passages (on standardless-discretion grounds). It does not address mass-arrest standards, individualized probable cause that each arrestee heard and willfully defied a dispersal order, or any requirement that a dispersal order be communicated person-by-person. Although the facts recite a sheriff's order to break up the demonstration, the decision turns on the constitutionality of the statutes, not on per-arrestee probable cause; that doctrine derives from the modern mass-arrest line the brief pairs it with, not from this opinion.

fields.brief_block: These protections apply to state and local government—including the Chicago Police—through the Fourteenth Amendment. Edwards v. South Carolina, 372 U.S. 229, 235 (1963).
fields.finding_analysis: Edwards directly supports the proposition: at the cited page the Court states that "these First Amendment freedoms are protected by the Fourteenth Amendment from invasion by the States," citing Gitlow, Whitney, Stromberg, De Jonge, and Cantwell, which is exactly the incorporation point the brief makes.

fields.brief_block: The Supreme Court's decision in Edwards v. South Carolina, 372 U.S. 229 (1963), is strikingly on point. [...] The Supreme Court unanimously reversed, holding: "The Fourteenth Amendment does not permit a State to make criminal the peaceful expression of unpopular views."
fields.finding_analysis: The quoted holding appears verbatim in the opinion ("The Fourteenth Amendment does not permit a State to make criminal the peaceful expression of unpopular views."), and the case did reverse the breach-of-peace convictions of peaceful protesters, so both the quotation and the proposition are accurate.

fields.brief_block: The Court stated that speech retains First Amendment protection even when it "induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction with conditions as they are, or even stirs people to anger." Id. at 237 (quoting Terminiello v. City of Chicago, 337 U.S. 1 (1949)). Only a "clear and present danger of a serious substantive evil" — one that "rises far above public inconvenience, annoyance, or unrest" — can justify suppression of otherwise protected assembly.
fields.finding_analysis: All three quoted phrases appear verbatim in Edwards, which quotes Terminiello v. Chicago for exactly this standard, and the brief accurately attributes the language to that quotation; the proposition that speech retains protection absent a clear and present danger of a serious substantive evil rising far above public inconvenience is squarely supported.

fields.brief_block: As the Supreme Court made clear in Edwards, police protection is "ample," a watching crowd is not itself threatening, and subjective police concern that a situation might deteriorate does not supply the clear and present danger of serious substantive harm needed to justify dispersing a peaceful assembly. 372 U.S. at 236.
fields.finding_analysis: Edwards supports each element of the proposition: the opinion finds "Police protection was ‘ample,’" that there was "no violence or threat of violence" by the protesters or any onlooker, and it discounts the City Manager's testimony that the crowd contained "possible trouble makers" who "could have been" trouble but were not, holding the convictions could not stand where the evidence showed only that the petitioners' views attracted a crowd and necessitated police protection.

fields.brief_block: The Supreme Court has held that speech "may best serve its high purpose when it induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction with conditions as they are, or even stirs people to anger." Edwards, 372 U.S. at 237.
fields.opinion_block: It may indeed best serve its high purpose when it induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction with conditions as they are, or even stirs people to anger.
fields.finding_analysis: The proposition is fully supported on substance — Edwards (quoting Terminiello) endorses precisely this idea — but the brief's quotation drops the word "indeed" from the opinion's "It may indeed best serve its high purpose," so the quoted string is not strictly verbatim even though the meaning is unchanged.

fields.brief_block: It sits at the zenith of constitutional protection under Hague v. CIO, Edwards v. South Carolina, and Gregory v. City of Chicago.
fields.opinion_block: The circumstances in this case reflect an exercise of these basic constitutional rights in their most pristine and classic form.
fields.finding_analysis: Edwards supports the core idea that peaceful political protest sits at the apex of constitutional protection — the Court called the petitioners' assembly on public government grounds an exercise of free-speech, assembly, and petition rights "in their most pristine and classic form." It does not, however, articulate "traditional public forum" doctrine or address a "downtown plaza"; that forum-specific framing is an extension of, rather than a holding in, Edwards, so the case partially but not fully supports the proposition as stated.

fields.brief_block: The "potential for escalation" justification is precisely the type of speculative risk that Edwards v. South Carolina held is constitutionally insufficient.
fields.opinion_block: protected against censorship or punishment, unless shown likely to produce a clear and present danger of a serious substantive evil that rises far above public inconvenience, annoyance, or unrest.
fields.finding_analysis: Edwards supports the proposition that a merely speculative risk cannot justify suppressing peaceful assembly: the Court required a "clear and present danger of a serious substantive evil," rejected reliance on testimony that onlookers were "possible trouble makers" who "could have been" trouble but were not, and reversed because the evidence showed only that the protesters' views attracted a crowd needing police protection. The quoted phrase "potential for escalation" is the brief's own label for the asserted justification and does not appear in the opinion, but the substantive point is well grounded.

fields.brief_block: The Court drew a careful distinction between Feiner v. New York, 340 U.S. 315 (1951) — where a speaker had genuinely incited near-riot conditions, the crowd was surging, and police authority was being actively challenged — and an ordinary peaceful protest attended by verbal disagreements.
fields.opinion_block: It is one thing to say that the police cannot be used as an instrument for the suppression of unpopular views, and another to say that, when as here the speaker passes the bounds of argument or persuasion and undertakes incitement to riot, they are powerless to prevent a breach of the peace.
fields.finding_analysis: Feiner v. New York is a free-speech/breach-of-peace case in which the Supreme Court affirmed a disorderly-conduct conviction, and its facts match the brief's characterization. The opinion recounts that Feiner urged his listeners "to rise up in arms and fight for equal rights," that the crowd was "restless" with "pushing, shoving and milling around" and "pressing closer around petitioner and the officer," that at least one onlooker "threatened violence if the police did not act," and that petitioner engaged in "deliberate defiance of the police officers" by refusing repeated orders. The Court expressly drew the very distinction the brief describes, contrasting "the ordinary murmurings and objections of a hostile audience," which "cannot be allowed to silence a speaker," with the situation "as here" where "the speaker passes the bounds of argument or persuasion and undertakes incitement to riot." The brief's use of Feiner as a near-riot, active-defiance contrast to an ordinary peaceful protest is a fair and accurate reading of the holding.
